comments:
Summary: The paper investigates fuzzy critical path analysis when activity durations are represented as discrete fuzzy sets. It formalises the target result via the extension principle (Proposition 1), then provides a concrete counterexample showing that commonly-used fuzzy forward recursion (using fuzzy addition and fuzzy maximum) can produce a membership function for the project completion time that is inconsistent with the extension principle. The paper identifies the source of the discrepancy (the fuzzy maximum comparing values coming from different configurations) and sketches two alternative approaches consistent with the extension principle (randomized sampling / Latin hypercube and a mathematical-programming formulation over the feasible range).

Strengths: The problem addressed is clearly stated and relevant to researchers and practitioners who apply fuzzy CPM. The use of the extension principle as a correctness criterion is appropriate. The counterexample is explicit and easy to follow; it convincingly demonstrates the failure mode. The discussion pinpoints the root cause (mixing values from different configurations when computing the fuzzy max), which is useful diagnostic insight. The paper cites and positions itself with respect to prior work and proposes constructive alternatives.

Weaknesses and suggestions: (1) Scope: the analysis is restricted to discrete-support fuzzy quantities; the paper should more explicitly delineate whether and when the forward recursion remains valid (e.g., for continuous fuzzy intervals or when certain dependence/independence assumptions hold). (2) Practical implications: the alternatives sketched are promising but only briefly described; more detail on computational cost, convergence behavior of the sampling approach, and tractability of the mathematical-programming method would strengthen the contribution. (3) Examples/evaluation: beyond the single illustrative counterexample, additional examples or a small experimental study comparing results and runtime of the proposed approaches would improve confidence in the practicality of the fixes. (4) Clarity/format: the manuscript contains some repetitive sentences and small editorial issues in the provided excerpt; tightening the presentation and clarifying notation (for instance, explicit assumptions about independence or joint possibility distributions) would help readers.

Recommendation: The paper makes a useful and correct technical point that challenges an implicitly assumed correctness of a popular algorithm for a commonly-used fuzzy representation. I recommend acceptance / dissemination after minor revision addressing clarity and expanding discussion of scope and computational aspects of the proposed remedies.
